original_abstract:
In an era defined by frequent and disruptive turbulence, the traditional pathways by which expert evidence informs government decision-making are under significant strain. This article serves as the introduction to a special issue exploring the evolving dynamics of policy advisory systems during major crises, with a primary focus on the COVID-19 pandemic and the "creeping" threat of climate change. We examine how the inherent uncertainty and urgency of crisis situations challenge the perceived objectivity of experts and reshape their institutional relationships with political leaders. 

By synthesizing diverse research contributions, this article highlights five critical factors influencing crisis governance: the necessity for high-speed, "real-time" scientific advice; the requirement for a broader, multidisciplinary diversity of expertise; the difficulties of managing stakeholder disagreement over core social values; the increasing pressure of politicization on independent advisory bodies; and the shifting public salience of experts as they move into "frontstage" communication roles. Further, we address the unique challenges faced by Low- and Middle-Income Countries (LMICs) regarding science infrastructure and the distinct strategies required to elevate slow-onset crises onto the political agenda. Collectively, these studies argue that robust crisis management requires moving beyond rigid, path-dependent structures toward more flexible, inclusive, and networked advisory systems capable of navigating high-stakes political and social trade-offs.
humanized_abstract:
It is not infrequent for politicians to express dissatisfaction with the conventional channels through which experts can bring their counsel to bear upon the formation of policy, due in part to the endemic and increasingly disruptif character of the turbulence to which policy makers are frequently subject. This editorial introduction sets out the background to a special issue focused upon the adaptation of policy advisory systems to periods of exceptional turbulence, exemplified by the COVID-19 pandemic and in the context of the “creeping” crisis of climate change. It sets out the uncertainty and sense of urgency that surround all crises, and considers how these inevitably affect the status and role of experts, as well as the nature of the institutional links that they cultivate with politicians.

This article compiles achievements from various projects that address the following factors considered key to crisis governance: - the need for rapid - “real time” scientific advice - the broadening of the knowledge base to include a variety of knowledge domains - dealing with potential conflicting world views in societies concerning core values - the politicization of scientific-advisory bodies; and - the performance of scientists in the limelight. In addition, this Special Issue addresses challenges encountered by Low- and Middle-Income Countries (LMICs) related to scientific capacity and deals with the particular strategies required to highlight slow-onnes crises on the political agenda. As a whole, the contributions to this Special Issue reinforce the idea that crisis governance goes beyond traditional bureaucratic responses and institutional set-ups. The complex and political character of many crisis situations demands more adaptive, inclusive and network-based scientific advisory structures, which can successfully accommodate highly politicized decisions and societal trade-offs.
